proxmox-mini-pc-nas-checklist.summary: A mini PC can be a strong Proxmox NAS host when the storage path, NIC, RAM, backups, and passthrough plan are checked before purchase.
facts: Do not buy on CPU alone; storage expandability and network reliability decide most NAS builds.
A supported 64-bit CPU, enough RAM, stable storage, and a reliable NIC are the baseline.
USB disks and tiny internal storage can work for labs but are weaker than planned SATA/NVMe layouts for important data.
Map the build to ZFS, backup, and passthrough pages before buying parts.
steps: List the workloads that must run: NAS VM, containers, firewall VM, media server, Home Assistant, or test VMs.
Check CPU virtualization support, RAM ceiling, NIC model, drive bays, M.2 slots, and cooling.
Decide whether storage stays inside Proxmox, passes through to a NAS VM, or lives on an external NAS.
Plan backups separately from the mini PC.
Prefer hardware with documented Linux and Proxmox community experience.
warnings: A compact mini PC can become storage-limited long before it becomes CPU-limited.
Do not treat RAID, ZFS mirror, or passthrough as a replacement for backups.
verification: The chosen system has enough physical storage connections for the intended NAS layout.
The NIC model is known and has a Linux driver path.
The backup destination is outside the same mini PC.
best_for: Buyers comparing N100, N305, Ryzen, and workstation-style mini PCs
not_for: Users who need vendor support for every component
common_mistakes: Putting the only backup on the same box

Passthrough needed: HBA / USB / GPU

faq: Is a mini PC enough for Proxmox?
Yes for many homelabs, but the fit depends on RAM, storage, NIC, cooling, and backup design more than branding.
Should I run TrueNAS as a VM?
Only if you understand the storage path and passthrough tradeoffs. Simple Proxmox storage can be better for small labs.

intel-n100-proxmox-nas.summary: Intel N100 mini PCs are attractive Proxmox NAS starters because they are low-power, modern, and inexpensive, but storage expansion and NIC quality decide whether the build is comfortable.
facts: Small Proxmox hosts running light VMs, containers, Home Assistant, media tools, and simple NAS duties.
Many N100 systems have limited internal storage expansion.
Confirm whether the unit uses Intel, Realtek, or another 2.5G NIC before buying.
Capacity and replaceability vary by model.
steps: Check the exact mini PC model rather than only the N100 CPU label.
Confirm RAM capacity, M.2 slots, SATA options, NIC model, and BIOS virtualization settings.
Decide whether the NAS workload needs ZFS mirror, external storage, or a separate NAS.
Plan a backup destination before storing important files.
Run a Proxmox installer test and verify network stability before migrating workloads.
warnings: N100 is efficient, not a substitute for drive bays.
USB-attached storage can be convenient but adds cabling and reliability tradeoffs.
verification: Proxmox installs cleanly.
The NIC survives reboot and sustained transfer tests.
VMs and containers fit within available RAM.
best_for: Small file services with external backup
not_for: Users needing ECC and server management features
common_mistakes: Assuming every 2.5G NIC behaves the same

Intel NICs are often preferred by homelab builders, but model-specific behavior still matters.
Validate link speed, stability, bridge configuration, and sustained transfers.
Network issues are often caused by cabling, switch negotiation, bridge config, or power saving rather than the NIC alone.

Check link speed and errors after installation.
Create or confirm the Linux bridge used by Proxmox guests.
Run sustained transfer tests to another 2.5G-capable device.
Record switch, cable, and BIOS settings so future issues are reproducible.
warnings: Do not assume two ports on the chassis mean identical NIC behavior.
A 2.5G NIC will not help if the switch, cable, or storage path is slower.
verification: The host links at expected speed.
Guests can reach the LAN through the bridge.
Sustained transfers do not flap the link.
not_for: Users who need guaranteed vendor support

A mirror costs half the raw capacity but protects against a single drive failure.
Drive count, cooling, and physical connection quality matter more in compact systems.

Decide whether Proxmox itself and VM data share the mirror.
Leave capacity headroom rather than filling the pool.
Schedule scrub and SMART monitoring where available.
Back up critical VM data to another system.

Do not run without an off-box backup.
verification: Both mirror members are visible.
A scrub completes without errors.
A restore test succeeds from backup.

hba-passthrough-truenas-vm.summary: HBA passthrough to a TrueNAS VM can work well when the host supports IOMMU, the controller is isolated cleanly, and Proxmox storage is separated from disks owned by the NAS VM.
facts: PCIe passthrough depends on hardware, firmware, IOMMU groups, and host configuration.
Do not let Proxmox and the NAS VM both manage the same disks.
A controller that exposes disks directly is usually preferred for NAS use.
Keep Proxmox boot and management independent from the passed-through storage controller.
steps: Confirm CPU, motherboard, and BIOS support IOMMU.
Check IOMMU groups before committing to the build.
Keep Proxmox boot storage outside the HBA passed to the NAS VM.
Pass through the controller, not individual disks, when the build calls for direct NAS ownership.
Test reboot, shutdown, backup, and recovery behavior before trusting the setup.
warnings: Passthrough is build-specific and can break with firmware or hardware changes.
A bad storage ownership design can put data at risk.
verification: The HBA appears in the VM.
The host does not use the passed-through disks.
A reboot returns storage and networking cleanly.
best_for: NAS VM builders with a dedicated HBA
not_for: First-time users who want the simplest storage path
common_mistakes: Passing through disks that Proxmox still depends on

New or uncommon NICs can add driver or kernel compatibility questions.
Network config changes can lock you out without local console access.
Check link, interface names, address, gateway, and bridge before changing hardware.
steps: Use local console access before editing network configuration.

Check which network interface name the host sees.

Verify static IP, gateway, DNS, and subnet.
Reboot only after saving known-good rollback notes.
warnings: Do not blindly copy another host's interface name.
Avoid remote-only network edits on a single-NIC host.
verification: The Proxmox web UI loads from another device.
The host can ping the gateway.
A VM attached to the bridge can reach the LAN.
not_for: Switch VLAN designs without topology notes
common_mistakes: Using the old eth0 naming assumption
Putting the IP on the physical interface instead of the bridge

Keep host management and boot paths independent from risky passthrough experiments.

Record the target device, VM owner, and what the host must keep for itself.
Check IOMMU grouping or USB device stability before committing.
Keep console access and rollback notes available.
Test reboot, shutdown, backup, and device reattachment behavior.
warnings: Passthrough can be hardware-specific and fragile across firmware or kernel changes.

decide whether Ceph fits mini PCs.
